paragraphs.body: The Scent of Nostalgia “MAMAAA!” An airy, childlike voice rang out from the hall, as tiny feet pitter-pattered down the hallway. “Mama, mama, mama!” I shouted melodiously in little spurts, as I flounced over towards the wooden bathroom door. “Yes, Booda?”, She purred lovingly, glancing over to me. There was my mama in all her glory, dressed in sleek black leather pants, adorned with an equally as stunning black leather jacket, and a simple white top to match. I looked up at her, as she took a carefully manicured hand to rummage through her makeup bag. She peered into the bag, stained with mysterious liquids and powders, and seemed to be deep in thought. I could tell from the furrow in her eyebrows, which was followed by a perplexed facial expression. All the while, I shimmied my way into the bathroom, and behind my mother’s shapely legs. Her other hand reached down to caress the top of my head. I angled myself on the edge of the smooth porcelain bathtub edge, swinging one of my legs over the side. My feet felt the residue of cold water, and I recoiled, swiping my feet towards a dry patch of tub. This was the perfect angle to watch mama. It seemed as if she hadn’t found her object yet- and she heaved heavily. “Booda, did you take my cherry gloss? I want to use it right now…” She fixed her eyes on me. Guiltily, I stood up, pitter-pattering towards my room, to grab the small tube-shaped container from my wind breaker pocket. “Here Mama…”, I handed the gloss to mama. She smiled, before reapplying her coat of gloss. “Thank you, baby.” She caressed my head, before continuing her private feminine affairs. In my little world, this is the hushed secret to being a black woman.

annotations.comment: As I said in your first draft: lovely narrative opening :)
annotations.context_left: her private feminine affairs. In my little world, this is the hushed secret to being a black woman.

paragraphs.body: My chosen object consists of a singular tube of cherry lip gloss. The outside is rounded, it’s long, and tubular shaped. Holding it in my hands, I can feel a thin layer of plastic separating the outer coating and the true surface of the tube of gloss. The top of the tube is a gold color and feels slightly sticky to the touch. The end of the container is fan shaped; its square edges dotted with tiny ridges alongside the ends. The text is printed, bold and a hot pink, “HYRDATING, Promotes NOURISHING, Contains MULTIVITAMINS.” The back displays the ingredients, chemicals, oils, and flavorings. “Ruby Kisses”, is fully displayed on the back, along with “CHERRY.” I refer to this gloss as “Black Girl Gloss”, as I mainly see it in hair stores in the Bronx and Brooklyn, the very same hair stores my mom would take me to pick up bobbles to tie into my small ponytails. The plastic is clear- you can see the gloss sliding around the insides. There would be many sets of this gloss nested in carboard containers, wedged between rat-tail combs, doo-go hair oil, tee-tree oil, and small jars of old sparkly nail polish that rarely anybody brought. When the cap is unscrewed, the balmy nostalgic scent of cherry wafts in the air. A bead Smalls 2 of gloss bubbles at the top, which I would apply quickly across my bottom lip. Sometimes, when I screw the cap back on, the gloss bubbles up at the sides of the cap. I would take my pinky, spreading it across my bottom lip. Nothing gets left behind. When I finished the application, the ritual would continue to commence. Now left with the task to evenly coat my lips in the gloss, but to a subtle extent- depending on the day, and the look. Once fully coated, I would find the nearest reflective surface, to inspect my handiwork. Eerily, I look like my mom from certain angles in my reflection. Once I was satisfied, the tube of nostalgia would be tucked into the nearest pocket of mine, to be secured until the next usage. Sometimes the container would be lost, perhaps from being shoved far within my bookbag pocket, or stuffed away in the corners of a purse. When hunting for my mother’s lip-gloss, her purse would be the first place I went to begin my hunt. I’d stuff my hand deep within the leather, plucking off various bits of debris and unwrapped gum sticks that crumbled, and were left to sediment at the bottom of the fabric lined bag. Sifting my small fingers around until I found the tube, the object. Then, I would inspect. Sometimes, the flavors would be mango, mint, strawberry, my mother liked options, she liked to be free to choose whatever she wanted. If I didn’t find the flavor I wanted, the gloss would be thrust back into the bag until further notice- I’d keep mental note of the location as a placeholder in case my hunt for the cherry flavor didn’t become fruitful, and continue my search.

annotations.text: refer to this gloss as “Black Girl Gloss”, as I mainly see it in hair stores in the Bronx and Brooklyn, the very same hair stores my mom would take me to pick up bobbles to tie into my small ponytails. The plastic is clear- you can see the gloss sliding around the insides. There would be many sets of this gloss nested in carboard containers, wedged between rat-tail combs, doo-go hair oil, tee-tree oil, and small jars of old sparkly nail polish that rarely anybody brought.

paragraphs.body: While choosing my word, blood, I found a clear definition to describe it. Blood, or ‘blod’ is a fluid that circulates in the arteries, and the veins. There’s the German word “blodam”, and the Old Norse-, “Bloo.” Other sources indicate bhlo-to, which means “to swell, gush, spurt.” These are all the interesting definitions I found. As for the etymological evolution of the word, “Blood”, I found that it was around since the 1950s- for bloodcurdling, an adjective- 1817. There also consists of the word ‘bloodletting’, which is an old medical practice of opening a vein to treat disease. The root word of blood is “bhel”, which is the European root meaning for ‘blooming, and thriving.’ Blood is also a word I associate with violence, specifically as it coats the walls in roughly shaped splatters.

The root word of blood is “bhel”, which is the European root meaning for ‘blooming, and thriving.’ Blood is also a word I associate with violence, specifically as it coats the walls in roughly shaped splatters.
annotations.comment: I wonder if you even need this paragraph? The next paragraph does the etymological work you need, I think.

paragraphs.body: The scent of cherries always lingered as my mother kissed my cheek, remaining even after her tears stained my sheets. When I was younger, I associated my home — my supposed sanctuary — with the smudged gloss of the walls, stained by her blood from the blows of my father's callused hands. I would be too scared to touch it, and would stare from the corner of the hallway, as if it burned a hole through the white plastered walls. Blood. Bloo, Bluh, Blood. Blood is an awfully striking word, isn’t it? It’s roots devolving from a Nordic background, something I don’t know much about. Old English plays a role into this as well- blod. Consisting of fluid flowing through the arteries and veins. It’s known as the ‘fluid of life’, in many cultures and backgrounds. So how come every time I’ve interacted with this ‘fluid of life’, have I experienced near death situations? If this word is so negative, how could there be any light in them? My mom, all beauty and grace, would leave her blood smeared on the walls, then wake up, broken and bruised, and paint on her gloss like armor, like none of it touched her. Is this still what it meant to be a black woman?

annotations.text: — my supposed sanctuary — with the smudged gloss of the walls, stained by her blood from the blows of my father's callused hands. I would be too scared to touch it, and would stare from the corner of the hallway, as if it burned a hole through the white plastered walls. Blood. Bloo, Bluh, Blood. Blood is an awfully striking word, isn’t it?

paragraphs.body: The suffix of Blood consists of “Bhel-“, which means to thrive, or to bloom. Despite my mother being flooded by violence, her resilience always shone through and grew. The tube of Smalls 3 cherry lip gloss I presume gave her comfort as much as it gave younger me comfort, for its scent reminded us both of better days, hot summer walks in the city, and the womanly primping of both of us in the mirror as we prepared ourselves to be presentable for the day. These days, whenever I have an important event, or just need a little confidence boost, my cherry lip gloss is the first thing I reach for. I swipe on my armor, bold and bright, and reapply whenever I need a hint of Black Girl Magic.

annotations.comment: Great revision! Excellent details and a powerful metaphor. I wonder, though, what does it mean for _you_ , specifically, to be proud and confident and powerful and brave? What is "black girl magic" in your life? What _does_ it mean to be a black woman? (In response to the question you asked). This is so fun to read. Thank you for sharing it!
